top10.introHtml: Top 10 picks for a cheap, fun second car near Media, PA — ranked by condition signals, model reliability, and fun factor. Mileage matters less than history and top condition on a convertible. Note: today’s refresh captured AutoTrader page 1 only (24 of 71 listings, $7,199–$11,500); Cars.com was blocked and CarGurus returned nationwide results.

picks.whyGood: The MR2 Spyder is a rare find: mid-engine, rear-wheel drive, 74K miles, no accidents, clean title, private seller. Toyota reliability in a genuine sports car package. These are underappreciated and underpriced — mid-engine balance, light weight, and a 5-speed manual make it the most rewarding driver's car in this entire batch.
picks.whatToVerify: MR2 Spyder tops are notoriously fussy — operate through a full open/close cycle and inspect fabric for tears, fraying at edges, and frame condition. Check for overheating history (1ZZ-FE can develop oil consumption if neglected). Inspect soft-top storage area behind seats for water damage or mold. Test clutch engagement and listen for 1ZZ oil burn at idle. Check rear-window seam.
picks.bottomLine: Mid-engine fun at 74K miles and under $10k — the MR2 Spyder rarely surfaces this clean and this affordable. Top pick.

picks.whyGood: The NC Miata is the gold standard for affordable, reliable, fun convertibles — these engines routinely go 250K miles with basic maintenance. 177K is barely middle-aged on a Miata. Grand Touring trim adds leather, Bose audio, and a more comfortable interior. At $11,450 with a dealer sale, this is exactly the kind of car that rewards long-term ownership.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate soft top through full cycle and inspect fabric and rear-window seam (NC top seals age well but check). Pull codes for any pending issues. Check power steering fluid (NC uses conventional hydraulic PS — watch for leaks). Inspect catalytic converter (theft-prone on NC Miata). Ask for service history especially timing chain and fluids.
picks.bottomLine: Best model for long-term reliability in this dataset — 177K on a Miata is nothing if it's been maintained.

picks.whyGood: The 2.8 is the Z3 you want — the inline-six transforms the car versus the anemic 1.9. 75K miles is low for a 2000 model. No accidents, clean title, private seller only 15 miles away. Under $8K for a genuine BMW roadster with the right engine is excellent value.
picks.whatToVerify: Check soft top fabric and rear plastic window for cracks and yellowing. Look under carpets for water intrusion at door seals. Inspect front subframe mounts (common Z3 issue). Oil leaks are common on M52/M54 engines — check valve cover and oil pan. Ask about cooling system refresh (thermostat, water pump, expansion tank).
picks.bottomLine: The best-value roadster in the batch — 2.8 inline-six, 75K miles, private-seller pricing under $8K.

picks.whyGood: 50K miles on a C4 Corvette convertible is almost unbelievably low — these tend to be well-cared-for cars. The LT1 V8 is a strong, serviceable engine. At $8,450 with verified no-accidents and clean title, this is exceptional value for an American icon. Private seller adds authenticity.
picks.whatToVerify: C4 rubber degrades with age regardless of miles — check all weatherstripping, top seals, and the rear-window plastic (often yellowed or cracked on 30-year-old cars). Test AC (C4s lose refrigerant). Check for rear differential whine. Inspect convertible top frame for binding or broken struts. Verify the top seals completely at highway speed.
picks.bottomLine: Fifty thousand miles on a Corvette convertible for $8,450 is a rare find — if the rubber has been tended to, this is a genuine sleeper deal.

picks.whyGood: The 986 Boxster is the closest thing to a genuine sports car in this price range — mid-engine, manual transmission, 74K miles, no accidents, clean title. The 2001 model uses the late-series M96 with a more robust IMS bearing design than the 1997-1999 cars. Only 11 miles away from Media. The Boxster's handling is simply in another class.
picks.whatToVerify: IMS bearing: the 2001 M96 uses a ball-bearing IMS — more reliable than the early roller-bearing units but still worth documenting. Get a pre-purchase inspection from a Porsche specialist who will pull the oil and inspect for metallic particles. Operate soft top fully — 986 tops can develop broken guide rails and motor failures. Check for oil leaks at RMS and coolant condition. Verify no check-engine lights.
picks.bottomLine: If the IMS PPI comes back clean, this is the most rewarding driver's car in the batch at a fair price — buy it.

picks.whyGood: The R52 MINI Cooper S is turbocharged go-kart fun with the lowest mileage of any MINI in this batch. No accidents reported. The S model's turbo makes it genuinely quick for a small car. Dealer-sold at Josie's Auto Sales for some accountability.
picks.whatToVerify: Check soft top fully — MINI convertible tops wear at rear corners and rear-window delamination is common. Check for coolant leaks (thermostat housing). Listen for timing chain rattle on cold start (R52 chain issues). Check for water leaks at door seals and top seal. Verify no check-engine lights. Ask about water pump history (electric, known MINI failure point).
picks.bottomLine: Lowest-mileage MINI in the batch with the S turbo trim — fun, practical, and dealer-sold at a fair price.

picks.whyGood: The Z4 is the Z3's more sophisticated successor — sharper chassis, better refinement, E46-derived suspension. The 2.5i inline-six is smooth and reliable. 88K miles, no accidents, sold by Mainline Auto (a local dealer) at 16 miles. Listed as a price drop.
picks.whatToVerify: Check the manual soft top operation (E85 Z4 uses a manual top unlike later models). Inspect rear window for delamination. Check for oil leaks from valve cover and oil filter housing. Common: coolant thermostat failure. Look at rocker-panel rust and lower door seam. Test all windows and electronics.
picks.bottomLine: Modern proportions and E46-derived chassis at Z3 prices — a strong buy if the soft top is in good shape.

picks.whyGood: The E93 328i is a retractable hardtop convertible — no fabric to worry about, no rear plastic window to crack. 80K miles is modest. Private seller with clean title. The N52 inline-six is a modern, refined unit. 15 miles away. The retractable metal roof is a genuine advantage for all-season usability.
picks.whatToVerify: Test the retractable hardtop mechanism multiple times — listen for grinding and watch for hesitation. Check the hydraulic fluid level and look for leaks at the cylinders. Inspect trunk seal for water intrusion. Get a Carfax (no accident badge on Autotrader). Check rear subframe mount bushings (E9X known wear item).
picks.bottomLine: A retractable metal roof eliminates the top-condition risk — solid modern drop-top at a fair private-seller price.

picks.whyGood: 62K miles on a 2007 A4 Cabriolet is exceptionally low — this car has barely been driven. No accidents. The B7 A4 Cabriolet is a sophisticated, refined drop-top that makes a surprisingly good daily driver. The 2.0T FSI engine is capable if maintained properly.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate the convertible top through a full cycle — B7 A4 Cabriolet uses a power soft top; check for hesitation or grinding. Inspect rear window and headliner for water staining. The 2.0T FSI can have carbon buildup on intake valves (direct injection) — ask about any service. Check for oil consumption (known 2.0T issue). Audi electrical gremlins are common — test all windows, lights, and HVAC.
picks.bottomLine: 62K miles on a B7 A4 Cabriolet is an unusual find — sophisticated and refined, but budget for Audi upkeep.

picks.whyGood: The SN-95 GT packs the 5.0L V8 (215 hp in 1995), and 55K miles is impressively low for a 30-year-old muscle car. No accidents gives confidence. Dealer-sold through GT Motors. This generation has a strong aftermarket and loyal community. At $10,495 it's a fair price for verified low mileage.
picks.whatToVerify: SN-95 convertible-specific: inspect the convertible-top-mount-to-rocker-pan area for rust (common structural rot point). Check the top mechanism and rear plastic window carefully. Test T-5 transmission by slow-shifting to third (synchro wear check). Rear axle clunk on takeoff is common. Check for water staining on rear seats from top seal failure. Verify no trunk floor rust.
picks.bottomLine: A low-mileage SN-95 GT convertible is rare — if the structure is solid and the top seals, this is solid V8 fun at a fair price.

mustangs.introHtml: One Ford Mustang convertible confirmed in the $7k–$15k range within 35 miles on page 1 of AutoTrader results. Ford had 6 total listings on AutoTrader but most are non-Mustang models (Thunderbird, etc.) or appear on page 2+ which wasn’t retrieved today.

picks.whyGood: The SN-95 GT packs the 5.0L V8 (215 hp in 1995), 55K miles is impressively low for a 30-year-old muscle car, and no accidents gives confidence. Dealer-sold through GT Motors. Strong aftermarket and community support. At $10,495, fair pricing for verified low mileage on a classic American V8 convertible.
picks.whatToVerify: SN-95 convertible-specific: inspect the convertible-top-mount-to-rocker-pan area for rust (common structural rot point on this generation). Check top mechanism and rear plastic window carefully. Slow-shift to third to test T-5 synchro. Check rear-axle clunk on takeoff. Inspect rear seats for water staining from top seal failure. Verify no trunk floor rust.
picks.bottomLine: A low-mileage SN-95 GT convertible is rare — solid structure plus a working top means solid V8 fun at a fair price.
